Govt Exams·GA
RBI Grade BUPSC / Banking News
← All UPSC / Banking material

UPSC / Banking · Reference source

Economic Survey 2025

9 sections summarised · 30 dedicated questions

01

Polity & Governance

  • Fiscal consolidation path – Union Budget FY 22 set a glide‑path to bring the fiscal deficit < 4.5 % of GDP by FY 26 (non‑binding annual targets).
  • Fiscal deficit fell from 9.2 % of GDP (FY 21) → 4.8 % (FY 25) → budgeted 4.4 % (FY 26).
  • Primary deficit (FD − Interest) is now largely zero, meaning current revenues cover non‑interest spending.
  • Debt‑to‑GDP target – Central government aims for 50 % ± 1 % by FY 31 (medium‑term anchor).
  • General‑government debt fell by ~7.1 pp since 2020, outperforming many peer economies.
  • Credit‑rating upgrades (2025) – Morningstar‑DBRS (May 2025), S&P (BBB‑→BBB, Aug 2025, first major upgrade in ~20 years), R&I (Sept 2025) citing deficit reduction, revenue buoyancy, growth‑enhancing capex, and fiscal transparency.
  • Four Labour Codes notified (Nov 21 2025) – consolidation of 29 central labour laws; key provisions: formal recognition of gig workers, portable social security, national floor‑wage ₹178‑500 daily, “Inspector‑cum‑Facilitator”, equal benefits for fixed‑term/contract workers after 1 yr.
  • GST‑2.0 reforms (effective 22‑Sep‑2025) – two‑rate structure: Standard 18 %, Merit 5 %, Sin‑good 40 %; major cuts: tractors 5 % (from 12 %), fertiliser acids 5 % (from 18 %), small cars 18 % (from 28 %), auto‑parts 18 % (from 28 %), electronics 18 % (from 28 %).
  • Radical overhaul of GST announced in FY 26 Budget – “most radical” since 2017.
  • Strategic governance narrative – Survey stresses an “entrepreneurial state” enabling mission‑mode platforms (e.g., semiconductors, green hydrogen).
  • State‑capacity emphasis – multiple boxes (e.g., Box I‑1, Box I‑2) underscore state‑capacity building as essential for strategic resilience.
  • PPP evolution – early‑2000s pilots → codified PPP framework for roads, ports, power, renewable energy; “3 P” (co‑design, early‑stage risk‑sharing, outcome‑based incentives).
  • Regulatory reforms – “Radical overhaul of GST”, “Four labour codes notified”, “Relaxation of green‑cover norms”.
02

Economy & Finance

  • Growth outlook – Potential real GDP growth revised to 7.0 % (up from 6.5 % three years ago); “Full‑year” growth > 7 % expected for FY 26 and near‑same level for FY 27.
  • FY 26 (2025‑26) real GDP – 7.4 % (First‑Advance Estimate); GVA growth 7.3 %; Services‑GVA share 53.6 % of GDP (H1 FY 26).
  • Private Final Consumption Expenditure (PFCE) – 61.5 % of GDP (H1 FY 26) – highest since FY 12; PFCE grew 7.5 % YoY in H1 FY 26.
  • Gross Fixed Capital Formation (GFCF) – 30 % of GDP (share) – 30.0 % (FAE); grew 7.6 % YoY in H1 FY 26; capex outlay ≈ ₹10.5 L cr (FY 20‑25).
  • Fiscal revenue – Direct‑tax share of total taxes 58.8 % (FY 25 PA); gross tax revenue 11.5 % of GDP.
  • Indirect taxes – GST receipts (Apr‑Dec 2025) ₹17.4 L cr, YoY +6.7 %; taxpayer base > 1.5 cr (2025); e‑way‑bill volume +21 % YoY.
  • Corporate tax collections – ₹4.58 L cr (FY 21) → ₹9.80 L cr (FY 25); corporate profits of listed firms +100 % (₹2.5 tr → ₹7.1 tr).
  • Government borrowing costs – 10‑yr bond yield = 6.7 % (rating BBB); Indonesia same rating yields 6.3 % (state‑level fiscal shadow).
  • External sector – Goods‑trade deficit persists; net services + remittance surplus insufficient to offset. Current‑account deficit H1 FY 26 0.8 % of GDP (down from 1.3 % FY 25).
  • Foreign‑exchange reserves – Cover >11 months of imports (as of 16 Jan 26); 94 % of external debt covered (Sept 25).
  • Cost of capital – Persistent current‑account deficits → risk premium on foreign savings; goal: shift to surplus‑generating economy to lower cost of capital durably.
  • Monetary policy – RBI cut policy rates aggressively early 2025; cumulative repo‑rate cut ≈ 125 bps (Feb 2025‑Nov 25); policy repo at 5.25 % (Dec 2025); CRR reduced ₹2.5 L cr, OMO ₹6.95 L cr, USD 25 bn forex swap.
  • Liquidity – Reserve‑money (M₀) growth 2.9 % YoY (Dec 2025); M₃ growth 12.1 % YoY; money‑multiplier 6.21 (up from 5.70).
  • Banking‑sector health – CRAR 17.2 % (Sept 2025); GNPA multi‑decadal low; NPA ratio 2.2 % (record low); NPA recovery 26.2 % FY 25; WALR on fresh loans ↓ 59 bps, on outstanding ↓ 69 bps.
  • Credit expansion – Non‑food bank credit +11.4 % YoY (Nov 25); non‑bank financing +29.3 % YoY (Apr‑Nov 25); corporate bond issuances +263.3 % YoY.
  • External debt – ≈ 2.6 % of GDP, mostly concessional; external‑debt‑to‑GDP 18.4 % (FY 24‑25).
  • Capital markets – Primary‑market mobilisation ₹10.7 L cr (FY 25‑26 to Dec 2025); equity issuances ₹1.6 L cr; corporate bond market > ₹53.6 trn outstanding (≈ 15‑16 % of GDP).
  • Insolvency & Bankruptcy Code (IBC) – S&P upgraded India’s insolvency regime Group C → Group B (3 Dec 2025); recovery rate ↑ to ≈30 %; resolution time ↓ to ~2 yr; resolution‑to‑liquidation ratio 91 % (FY 25).
03

International Relations

  • US‑India tariff clash – April 2025: US President announced reciprocal 25 % tariff on Indian goods; August 2025: additional 25 % penal tariff on most Indian merchandise exports (effectively 50 % on many items).
  • EU‑India FTA – concluded after three‑year talks; pending ratification by the European Parliament; expands market access for labour‑intensive manufactured exports and deepens integration with European technology/manufacturing.
  • India‑UK CETA – signed July 2025; provides access to 137 services sub‑sectors, MRAs in nursing, accountancy, architecture; expected to lift FDI inflows.
  • India‑Israel BIT – signed 2025 (investment protection).
  • India‑EFTA TEPA – binding USD 100 bn investment commitment and 1 m jobs (signed Oct 2025).
  • Geopolitical risk index – Lowy Institute Power‑Gap score –4.0, lowest in Asia (excl. Russia & NK). Indicates India operates below full strategic potential.
  • Friend‑shoring & Near‑shoring – UNCTAD (2025) shows “Friend‑shoring Index” above 2021 average; “Near‑shoring Index” below 2021 average but modest rise Q3 2025.
  • EU Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism (CBAM) – tariffs on steel & cement based on embedded emissions; affects Indian exporters.
  • GIFT City – International Financial Services Centre (IFSC) regulated by IFSCA (est. Apr 2020); 1,034 registered entities (2025); Global Financial Centres Index rank 43/120 (↑ 9 places).
04

Environment & Ecology

  • Green‑cover norm for industries – uniform 33 % requirement replaced by a pollution‑potential‑based norm (2025).
  • Quality‑Control Orders (QCOs) – “Indiscriminate QCOs … were put on hold” (2025); 46 QCOs (51 products) revoked; 3 QCOs (63 products) deferred/suspended.
  • Power‑Gap Index (Lowy Institute) – Score ‑4.0, lowest in Asia (excl. Russia & N K).
  • Carbon‑Credit Trading Scheme (CCTS) – launched June 2023; mandatory compliance for aluminium, cement, chlor‑alkali, pulp & paper; voluntary offset in 10 sectors.
  • Environmental‑regulation shift – relaxed green‑cover norm; CCTS introduced.
  • Renewable Energy – Non‑fossil‑fuel (NFF) power share 51.93 % of installed capacity (Dec 2025); Solar 135.81 GW, Wind 54.51 GW, total RE 253.96 GW (49.83 % of total capacity).
  • ESG debt securities – SEBI (2025) framework for ESG‑linked debt (other than green bonds); GIFT City listings already include USD 15 bn ESG securities.
05

Science & Technology

  • AI ecosystem – Chapter 14 “Evolution of the AI ecosystem in India”; AI‑driven services boost export growth by ≈39.5 % for AI‑intensive services; software services export growth +13.5 % (FY 23‑25).
  • Data‑centre capacity – ≈1.4 GW (Q2 2025); target ≈8 GW by 2030; > 70 % of global data‑centres (by count) are in High‑Income Countries (June 2025).
  • Generative AI start‑ups – 890 active (H1 FY 25) vs 240 (H1 FY 24); funding ≈ US$ 990 mn.
  • Space sector – Valuation US$ 8.4 bn (≈ 2 % of global space market) → projected US$ 44 bn by 2035; NSIL revenue FY 20 ₹322 cr → FY 23 ₹2,940 cr → FY 25 projected ₹3,246 cr.
  • Satellite‑data services market – US$ 495 m (2024).
  • Quantum‑secured communication – under development; linked to indigenous 4G/5G core‑networks.
  • Renewable‑energy storage – Battery Energy Storage Systems (BESS) policy incentives; VGF funding for ≈43 GWh (2024‑25, 2025‑26).
  • Advanced GIS for spring mapping – > 55,000 springs mapped nationwide (2025).
06

Government Schemes & Social Sector

  • VB‑G‑Ram G Bill 2025 – “Viksit Bharat Guarantee for Rozgar & Ajeevika Mission”; replaces MGNREGA; legal guarantee 125 days per rural household; weekly wage disbursement.
  • Panchayat Advancement Index (PAI) – composite tool for 25 lakh Gram Panchayats; 435 unique local indicators; tracks SDG progress.
  • PM‑Kisan – ₹6 000/year income support; total outlay ₹4.09 L cr (21 instalments).
  • PM‑KMY – voluntary pension for cultivators; 24.92 lac enrolments (31 Mar 2025).
  • PM‑Kisan Bima – PMFBY coverage 4.19 crore farmers (↑ 32 % YoY); claims paid ₹1.90 L cr since 2016‑17.
  • Pradhan Mantri Krishi Sinchai Yojana (PMKSY) – irrigated area share ↑ 41.7 % (2001‑02) → 55.8 % (2022‑23); 55 % subsidy to marginal farms, 45 % to others.
  • Rural Development – PMGSY connectivity 99.7 % of eligible habitations (Dec 2025); PM‑JANMAN targeting 8 000 km of tribal roads (2023‑28).
  • Skill Vouchers – demand‑side financing allowing trainees to choose institutes; Maharashtra Vikalp programme shows 60 % job‑retention.
  • Apprenticeship Promotion Scheme (NAPS) – 43.47 lakh apprentices across 36 states/UTs; female participation 20 %.
  • National Mission on Edible Oils (NMEO‑OS) – target ≈ 70 MT oilseed production by FY 30‑31; imports share down from 63.2 % (2015‑16) → 56.25 % (2023‑24).
  • National Agricultural Extension (RKVY‑SAME) – 39.04 lac farmers benefited (2024‑25).
  • e‑NAM – virtual mandis with 1.79 cr farmers, 2.72 L cr traders, 4,698 FPOs (2025).
  • Digital Agriculture Mission (Sept 2024) – AgriStack, Krishi Decision‑Support System, soil‑fertility maps; rollout ongoing.
  • Cash‑Plus Model – Rajasthan – integrates DBT with behaviour‑change communication for nutrition; pilot in 5 tribal districts shows +54 % women using cash for nutrition, +30 % for food, +35 % myth reduction.
07

Geography & Places in News

  • Airport network – doubled over the past decade (≈ 164 airports in 2025 vs 74 in 2014).
  • Inland waterways – freight movement has rapidly grown, easing logistics constraints.
  • GIFT City (Gandhinagar, Gujarat) – India’s first IFSC; 1,034 registered entities; rank 43/120 in Global Financial Centres Index (2025).
  • Kochi Water Metro – 15 routes, 78 km, ₹819 cr cost; 100 % of 5.43 lac FPS equipped with e‑PoS.
  • Kolkata‑Bangalore High‑Speed Rail corridor – part of 5,364 km operational high‑speed network (FY 25).
  • Dedicated Freight Corridors – Eastern (1,337 km) fully complete; Western (1,404 km) 93 % complete (Oct 2025).
  • National Waterway‑1 (Varanasi‑Haldia) – 1,390 km; cargo ↑ 220 % (5.05 → 16.38 MMT).
  • Odisha – 39 k “Pani Panchayats”, 55 k spring maps, integrated irrigation projects.
  • Kerala – Services‑share 64.3 % of GVA; tourism “Medical & Wellness” segment > 6 lakh arrivals (2022‑24).
08

History, Art & Culture

  • *No specific historical, artistic or cultural facts were provided in the source chunks.*
09

Awards, Sports & Miscellaneous

  • Power‑Gap Index (Lowy Institute) – Score ‑4.0, lowest in Asia (excl. Russia & N K).
  • Gold price 2025 – rose from USD 2,607 to USD 4,315 per ounce (reflecting global risk aversion).
  • Global Economic Policy Uncertainty Index – near‑worst readings since 2020; spike +1 165.6 % YoY (April 2025).
  • Rupee valuation – described as “punching below its weight” – undervalued relative to fundamentals; cushions India from higher import‑tariff impacts and curbs inflation from expensive crude imports.
  • Sports – No specific sports data in the supplied chunks.
★

Practice

30 exam-style questions drawn from Economic Survey 2025. Pick an answer to check it.
